findings.title: MFA code generated with non-cryptographic Math.random()
findings.fix_sketch: Replace Math.random() with crypto.randomInt(100000, 1000000). The verify endpoints should also use crypto.timingSafeEqual(Buffer.from(stored), Buffer.from(code)) to avoid timing oracles.

findings.title: MFA code stored in Redis as plaintext (no hashing)
findings.evidence: await redis.setex(key, 300, code); // 5 minutes TTL — code stored as plaintext string in Redis.
findings.fix_sketch: Hash the code before storing (bcrypt or HMAC-SHA256 with a server-side secret) and compare hashes on verify. Defense-in-depth against Redis memory dumps or backup leakage. Note: rate limit and 5-min TTL make this low risk today.

findings.title: MFA code is 6 digits — brute-forceable in theory
findings.evidence: Code space: 10^6 = 1,000,000 values. Rate limit 5/min per IP per scope = 12,000 attempts/hour. With ~83 hours of effort an attacker could enumerate all codes. In practice the 5-min TTL and email-out-of-band make this low risk.
findings.fix_sketch: Increase code length to 8 digits (10^8 space) or use a 32-char alphanumeric token. Alternatively, lock the account after 10 failed MFA attempts within 5 minutes.
